title: Mutability, aliases and copying

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapter 5: lists, tuples, sets, dictionaries, looping, and sequence comparison; atomic topic: Mutability, aliases and copying
sources.claim: Python assignment copies a reference binding rather than an object; shallow copying duplicates one container shell, while nested values remain shared unless copied or rebuilt intentionally.

sources.claim: Python assignment copies a reference binding rather than an object; shallow copying duplicates one container shell, while nested values remain shared unless copied or rebuilt intentionally.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python assignment copies a reference binding rather than an object; shallow copying duplicates one container shell, while nested values remain shared unless copied or rebuilt intentionally.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: After b = a, b.append changes the one shared list; b = list(a) creates a separate outer list but still aliases nested dictionaries. Your goal is to trace and repair accidental shared mutation in nested collections. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: for, while and loop control; pf data selection; Lists, tuples and slicing; pf state mutation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python assignment copies a reference binding rather than an object; shallow copying duplicates one container shell, while nested values remain shared unless copied or rebuilt intentionally. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Immutable values can be safely shared, deepcopy may copy too much or fail on resources, default arguments persist across calls, and in-place methods differ from rebinding. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Assigning one list to another name creates an independent list, and list.copy recursively detaches nested values.

includes: Distinguish shared references, shallow copies and independent nested values.
Trace and repair accidental shared mutation in nested collections.
excludes: Immutable values can be safely shared, deepcopy may copy too much or fail on resources, default arguments persist across calls, and in-place methods differ from rebinding.

outcomes: Trace and repair accidental shared mutation in nested collections.

examples.title: Mutability, aliases and copying: direct language case
examples.situation: After b = a, b.append changes the one shared list; b = list(a) creates a separate outer list but still aliases nested dictionaries.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: After b = a, b.append changes the one shared list; b = list(a) creates a separate outer list but still aliases nested dictionaries.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Trace object identity and aliases explicitly: assignment shares the object and a shallow copy duplicates only the outer container.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Trace object identity and aliases explicitly: assignment shares the object and a shallow copy duplicates only the outer container.

examples.title: Mutability, aliases and copying: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A nested configuration is rebuilt field by field because an indiscriminate deepcopy would duplicate identity-bearing services incorrectly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A nested configuration is rebuilt field by field because an indiscriminate deepcopy would duplicate identity-bearing services incorrectly.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Immutable values can be safely shared, deepcopy may copy too much or fail on resources, default arguments persist across calls, and in-place methods differ from rebinding.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Assigning one list to another name creates an independent list, and list.copy recursively detaches nested values.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace and repair accidental shared mutation in nested collections.

limitations: hashability, shallow copying, Unicode normalization, and object identity require Python's exact data model
